title: Add spawnable agent classes and reserve orchestrator for root session
description:
Introduce an explicit distinction between spawnable child agent classes and the root orchestrator role. The interactive Pi session becomes the orchestrator via /orchestrate; users should not create/spawn child agents that pretend to be orchestrators. Proposed spawnable classes: lead, scout, implementer, reviewer. Reserve orchestrator as a non-spawnable root/session role.

Root orchestrator profile direction: support root-only skills/instructions/prompts from Orchestrator Libraries, but do NOT auto-load arbitrary root extensions from libraries. The root shell already runs the pi-agent-orchestrator extension; additional root extension behavior should come from normal trusted Pi extension installation or a fork/custom package. Library extensions remain for spawned agents via --extension.

Acceptance direction:
- Agent definitions support an explicit class/category field for spawnable roles.
- Dashboard create/edit flows use a class picker and do not offer orchestrator as spawnable.
- Backend spawn paths reject reserved/non-spawnable orchestrator definitions even if manually edited.
- Package pio-example-orchestrator is hidden from spawnable agent type lists or recast as root-profile/example documentation.
- Skill templates and possibly skills can carry audience/scope metadata such as spawned/orchestrator/all.
- Capability resolution refuses orchestrator-only resources for child agents.
- Root orchestrator profiles can contribute skills/instructions/prompts, likely through reload/resource discovery or before_agent_start injection.
- Root orchestrator profiles do not load library extension files into the interactive shell.
- Root orchestrator access model is documented separately from child --skill/--extension injection.
closeReason: Verified spawnable agent classes epic acceptance is implemented: agent definitions parse/save class metadata, dashboard create/edit uses spawnable-only class picker, backend/API/tool/spawn paths reject orchestrator-class definitions, package orchestrator example is non-spawnable, template/skill audience guardrails and root profile capability resolution are in place, README/CONTEXT document root profiles separately, and bun run check passes.

title: Implement root orchestrator profiles for skills and instructions
description:
Follow-up under pi-agent-orchestrator-9c53. Add a root orchestrator profile concept for the interactive /orchestrate session. Profiles may contribute root-only skills/prompts/instructions from Orchestrator Libraries, likely via persisted orchestration mode/profile selection plus ctx.reload/resources_discover and/or before_agent_start injection. Do not load arbitrary library extension files into the root shell.

Design notes from discussion:
- The package should always ship/discover a built-in root profile named `default`; there should never be a no-profile state.
- Users may copy/create additional root profiles later.
- `/orchestrate <profile>` should activate the named root orchestrator profile without prompting.
- If `/orchestrate` is run with no profile argument and exactly one root profile exists, activate that profile automatically. In practice this will usually be the built-in `default` profile.
- If `/orchestrate` is run with no profile argument and multiple root profiles exist, do not pick default/first silently; ask the user to select which profile to use.
- Root profiles are for orchestrator skills/instructions only, not arbitrary extension loading.

Acceptance: /orchestrate can activate a selected/default root profile; profile instructions/skills are available to the root session after activation/reload; root profile behavior is documented separately from spawned child --skill/--extension injection; multiple-profile no-argument flow prompts for selection instead of assuming a default; built-in `default` root profile is always available.
closeReason: Implemented root orchestrator profiles. Added built-in default profile, profile discovery from package/user/project/Orchestrator Library directories, target-aware profile capability resolution, /orchestrate profile activation with multi-profile selection, persisted profile state across reloads, resources_discover skill injection, before_agent_start instruction injection, Orchestrator Library starter scaffold support, and README docs. Validation: bun run check passes (87 tests).

title: Add audience guardrails for orchestrator-only skills and templates
description:
Follow-up under pi-agent-orchestrator-9c53. Add audience/scope metadata for skills and skill templates such as spawned/orchestrator/all, then enforce that orchestrator-only resources cannot be attached to spawned child agents.

Design notes from discussion:
- `applyToAll` currently means auto-apply to newly spawned agents; rename/represent this more explicitly as auto-apply to all spawned agents.
- Keep audience/eligibility separate from auto-application.
- Skill templates should support audience/eligibility for spawned agents, root orchestrator, or both.
- Skill templates should support automatic application modes: manual/specific assignment only, all spawned agents, or all/everywhere including orchestrator.
- The UI should use a radio/select-style control rather than one ambiguous boolean checkbox.
- Specific agent-type assignment remains done from the agent type editor by assigning a skill template.
- Backend validation must enforce impossible states even if bypassing the UI/API clients.
- Extension templates remain spawned-agent-only for now; no root orchestrator extension loading.

Acceptance: dashboard labels/filtering make orchestrator-only resources clear; template save/edit supports audience metadata; capability resolution refuses orchestrator-only skills/templates for child spawn; root orchestrator profiles may use orchestrator-only skills/instructions; tests cover manual markdown bypass attempts.

title: Containerized orchestration runtime and issue handoff artifacts
description:
Capture the planned shift toward a simpler trusted-local orchestration model.

Resolved direction:
- Docker/devcontainer becomes the outer reproducible Pi orchestration environment for local use and cross-machine/Windows-friendly development.
- Pi runs inside the container; spawned agents run as normal child Pi RPC processes inside the same container. Do not add per-agent bwrap or nested Docker subcontainers.
- This repo is still a prototype, so do not preserve bwrap as a legacy fallback unless a future need appears. Prefer removing bwrap-specific code paths and simplifying spawn.
- Keep git worktrees as the primary boundary for issue/task implementation and handoff.
- Use container paths as runtime truth. Host paths are only Docker/devcontainer launch configuration.

Auto-discovered Orchestrator Library model:
- Orchestrator Libraries are discovered automatically from bounded manifest locations; users should not manually register arbitrary library paths in the dashboard.
- Repo-local libraries must live under the app-owned project folder, e.g. `.pi/pi-agent-orchestrator/libraries/<library-name>/orchestrator-library.json`.
- External library repos must be bind-mounted into the app-owned external library folder before Pi starts, e.g. `.pi/pi-agent-orchestrator/external-libraries/<library-name>/orchestrator-library.json` inside the container workspace.
- Discovery should scan those manifest locations only, then validate schema/name/compatibility metadata before loading resources.
- Repo-local libraries are discovered first, then mounted external libraries in deterministic path/name order. Do not add load-order/reorder UI for now.
- If enabled libraries conflict, surface diagnostics and require the user to disable one of the conflicting libraries; do not silently choose a winner for conflicting namespaces/resources.
- External mounted libraries are allowed to be writable: users may want agents to create/update/delete resources in those mounted repos.
- New library creation from the dashboard should create repo-local libraries only under `.pi/pi-agent-orchestrator/libraries/<library-name>/`; external libraries require Docker/devcontainer bind-mount setup outside the running dashboard.
- Orchestrator Library extension code runs from the discovered container path. Library/runtime dependencies must be installed inside the container, so setup scripts should bootstrap discovered/mounted libraries that contain dependency manifests.
- The external-libraries mount folder should be ignored by normal project git status unless the user intentionally changes ignore policy, because its contents belong to separately versioned repos.

Agent lifecycle and context model:
- Root orchestrator should stay light on context. It owns lifecycle management, issue allocation, health/log monitoring, doom-loop detection, final review, and durable metadata promotion.
- Root starts a Lead Agent for an issue/task and gives it the task context.
- Lead Agent owns issue-level context gathering and execution coordination: it requests scouts/researchers/builders, gathers repo/web context, prepares builder handoff artifacts, and synthesizes final results.
- Scouts/researchers should be tool-restricted for investigation only. Builders should receive implementation-ready context and focus on execution.
- Handoff artifacts are operational context bundles created by agents for agent-to-agent continuity; they are not the same as durable Mulch knowledge or Seeds tracker updates.
- Root may review artifact summaries and promote selected outcomes into Seeds/Mulch, but should not ingest all raw agent context.

Tool/capability constraints discovered during planning:
- Pi supports tool allowlisting via `--tools <list>` across built-in, extension, and custom tools.
- Built-in tools can be removed from a role by omitting them from the allowlist, e.g. a read-only scout can get read/grep/find/ls but no edit/write.
- Current spawn code has a caveat: `buildPiArgs()` skips `--tools` whenever extra extensions are loaded, and it always adds `delegate`. This blocks strict role lockdown for agents that need third-party tools. Fix this early so agent types/templates can combine selected built-ins with selected extension/custom tools.

Revised tracer bullets:
1. Docker/devcontainer baseline: add official Dockerfile/devcontainer plus setup notes for project workspace, Pi config/cache/auth persistence, and optional bind mounts into `.pi/pi-agent-orchestrator/external-libraries/*`.
2. Direct process agent launch: remove bwrap dependency from spawn; launch child Pi RPC processes directly with `cwd = worktreePath`; remove `/tmp/workspace` path translation.
3. Capability allowlist correctness: allow agent types/templates to specify exact built-in plus extension/custom tools; do not auto-add `delegate`; keep `--tools` active even when extensions are loaded.
4. Auto-discovered Orchestrator Libraries: scan `.pi/pi-agent-orchestrator/libraries/*/orchestrator-library.json` and `.pi/pi-agent-orchestrator/external-libraries/*/orchestrator-library.json`, expose enable/disable state, validate conflicts, and remove arbitrary path registration/reorder concepts.
5. Orchestrator Library bootstrap in container: create new libraries only in the repo-local app folder, and add setup/install flow for discovered libraries with dependency manifests.
6. Issue handoff artifact skeleton: standardize `.pi/pi-agent-orchestrator/issues/<issue-id>/` and initial lead/scout/researcher/builder artifact filenames without requiring full protocol gates yet.
7. Lead-centered handoff prompts/templates: update lead/scout/researcher/builder role instructions so leads request specialists, scouts report concise dossiers, builders consume prepared handoffs, and root receives summaries only.
8. Dashboard/runtime visibility: surface launch mode, discovered libraries, active worktree/artifact paths, disposable test session status, and warnings when desired external libraries are not mounted.

Deferred hardening follow-up:
- Track future web/network guardrails in pi-agent-orchestrator-655c. This should not block the current Docker/process migration. The likely direction is a companion Pi guardrail extension around pi-web-access plus container/proxy network enforcement so web_search/fetch_content/code_search can be policy-audited and raw bash egress cannot bypass those policies.

Relationship to protocol epic:
- This Docker/process migration should happen before Orchestration Protocol Mode.
- Protocol mode can later enforce packet-gated handoffs using the simplified process launch model, exact tool allowlists, mounted library discovery, and the issue artifact path established here.
closeReason: Closed as historical/foundational runtime planning. Remaining active orchestration/runtime behavior should be driven by pi-agent-orchestrator-0403 Workflow/WorkflowRun design rather than this older root/lead handoff architecture.

title: Extension template smoke test should fail when no runtime tools are active
description:
Smoke-testing an Extension Template can report `smoke passed` even when runtime diagnostics say `Runtime reported 0 active tools and 19 total tools` and the Tools line says `none reported`. For extension templates, a pass with zero active extension tools is misleading: the test should verify that at least one requested extension contributed active tools, or at minimum fail/warn when no active tools are available.

Observed example: a test extension template containing pi web access and Seeds CLI reported smoke passed while runtime diagnostics showed no active tools.

Investigate:
- Check extension-smoke-test runtime criteria in extensions/multi-agent/extension-smoke-test.ts.
- Confirm whether the temporary smoke-test agent actually spawns and what agent definition/model/runtime is used.
- Inspect runtime-tools snapshot semantics: active vs all tools.
- Surface enough diagnostics/logs in the dashboard to know which smoke-test agent was spawned and why no tools became active.

Acceptance:
- Smoke test fails or at least reports a clear error when requested extensions load zero active runtime tools.
- Diagnostics identify the smoke-test agent id/type/runtime path enough to debug startup.
- Tests cover zero-active-tools behavior.
- bun run check passes.
closeReason: Extension template smoke tests now fail when a runtime snapshot reports zero active tools, include smoke-test agent id/definition/worktree diagnostics, and log spawn/runtime snapshot details. Dashboard runtime diagnostics display the smoke-test agent. Added API coverage for zero-active-tools behavior. Validation: bun run check passes (91 tests).

When the user clicks Smoke Test, the temporary smoke-test agent should use the same model as the current interactive/root orchestrator session when available, so credential/model availability matches what the user is actively using. The dashboard should display the model used for the smoke-test agent along with agent id/definition/worktree.

Context: smoke diagnostics currently show the temporary agent id and worktree, but not the model. The user observed failure with requested extensions (`pi-web-access/index`, `@os-eco/seeds-cli/index`) and wants to know whether the failure is caused by the smoke-test agent using a different/default model with missing credentials.

Investigation notes:
- Pi extension docs expose `ctx.model` and `model_select` events.
- startServer deps can carry a currentModel provider function from the root extension.
- smokeTestExtensionTemplate can pass `model` into spawnAgent and include it in smokeAgent diagnostics.

Acceptance:
- Smoke-test spawn uses current root model pattern when known.
- Runtime diagnostics show model used, plus agent id/definition/worktree.
- If model is unknown, diagnostics clearly say default model selection was used.
- Tests cover model propagation to smoke-test spawn and result diagnostics.
- bun run check passes.

Added model propagation assertions in smoke-test API coverage. Validation: bun run check passes (91 tests).

title: Investigate extension smoke tests reporting zero active tools for real package extensions
description:
Extension Template smoke tests now correctly fail when the runtime snapshot reports zero active tools, but real package extensions still appear to load with zero active tools in practice.

Observed diagnostics:
- Agent: `smoke-test-1779227701578` (`extension-smoke-test`)
- Model: `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`
- Worktree: `/tmp/pi-worktree-smoke-test-1779227701578-1779227701581`
- Requested extensions: `pi-web-access/index`, `@os-eco/seeds-cli/index`
- Runtime snapshot: `0 active tools and 19 total tools`
- Tools line: `none reported`

Questions to investigate:
1. Are `pi-web-access/index` and `@os-eco/seeds-cli/index` actually being resolved to extension file paths and passed to child Pi via `--extension`?

Acceptance:

title: Epic: Orchestration Protocol Mode with packet-gated agent handoffs
description:
Build a strict packet-based orchestration protocol so agents do not start from zero and cannot bypass required handoff/feedback artifacts.

Motivation:
- Pi Lattice orchestration mode gives us a hierarchy: root orchestrator -> lead -> scout/builder/reviewer roles.
- Context retrieval should leverage that hierarchy instead of requiring every spawned agent to ls/rg from scratch.
- The protocol should be enforced at operation boundaries with Pi hooks/tools, not merely described in prompts.
- Defer implementation until after the planned Docker/devcontainer migration replaces the current bwrap-first spawning model.

Corrections/constraints:
- Do not load/build the Repo Atlas on normal Pi startup. Only prepare/load it when the user explicitly enables orchestration mode, e.g. via /orchestrate or an equivalent orchestration activation event.
- The lead is not an orchestrator and should not receive broad orchestration/spawn tools. Root orchestrator owns process lifecycle and durable orchestration state. Leads may produce plans/requests/packets, but spawning should be root-mediated or protocol-mediated by a constrained tool.
- Project-local artifacts created by this extension should live under the app-owned `.pi/pi-lattice/` folder, not loose `.pi/orchestrator/` paths.

Core invariant in protocol mode:
- No agent may be spawned or sent work without a valid packet.
- No scout may finish without submitting an Area Dossier.
- No builder may mutate files without a valid Builder Packet.
- No builder may finish without a Completion Report.
- Raw free-form agent_send / agent_spawn should be blocked or hidden in protocol mode except for explicit debug/manual escape hatches.

Primary artifacts:
- Repo Atlas: broad repo routing map for the root orchestrator only. Generated/loaded when orchestration mode is enabled, not at Pi startup.
- Issue Context Packet: root orchestrator -> lead packet with likely areas, relevant Mulch/Seeds context, read-order hints, unknowns, constraints, and validation.
- Lead Plan / Work Request: lead -> root protocol artifact requesting scout/builder/reviewer work. Lead does not directly become an orchestrator.
- Scout Packet: narrowly scoped investigation request.
- Area Dossier: scout -> lead/root findings with evidence, read files, exact functions/modules, risks, unknowns, and recommended builder packet material.
- Builder Packet: implementation-ready packet with files, change plan, acceptance criteria, validation, and constraints.
- Completion Report: builder -> lead/root result with changed files, validation run, remaining risks, and context-index/Mulch promotion candidates.

Implementation shape:
- Add extensions/multi-agent/protocol/ modules:
  - types.ts: schemas for RepoAtlas, IssueContextPacket, LeadPlan/WorkRequest, ScoutPacket, AreaDossier, BuilderPacket, CompletionReport.
  - store.ts: persist/read artifacts under `.pi/pi-lattice/` and/or session custom entries.
  - tools.ts: register protocol tools.
  - gates.ts: Pi hook enforcement for illegal tool calls.
  - prompts.ts: role-specific prompt fragments and packet rendering.
- Register protocol hooks in extensions/multi-agent/index.ts:
  - /orchestrate activation path loads/builds Repo Atlas and protocol state.
  - before_agent_start injects active root profile + protocol requirements only when orchestration mode is active.
  - tool_call blocks raw agent_send/agent_spawn/create_sub_agent bypasses and role-inappropriate mutations.
  - tool_result records read/edit/bash evidence for packet/dossier/report artifacts.
  - agent_end detects missing required final artifacts and steers/fails closed where possible.
- Spawn child agents with role-specific tools and protocol extension/prompt:
  - scout: read, constrained bash/search, submit_area_dossier. No edit/write.
  - builder: read, bash, edit, write, submit_completion_report, but edits blocked until Builder Packet is accepted/present.
  - lead: analysis/planning/reporting tools only; no broad create_sub_agent/agent_spawn/agent_send. It can submit scout/builder work requests or packet recommendations for the root/protocol layer to execute.
- Use packet files as the context product:
  `.pi/pi-lattice/issues/<issue-id>/`
    issue-context.json
    lead-plan.json
    scouts/<scout-id>.packet.json
    scouts/<scout-id>.dossier.json
    builders/<builder-id>.packet.json
    builders/<builder-id>.completion.json

Suggested tracer bullets:
1. Add protocol artifact schemas/store and a prepare_issue_context tool that writes an Issue Context Packet.
2. Add /orchestrate activation behavior that loads/builds Repo Atlas lazily, not during normal startup.
3. Gate raw agent_send/agent_spawn in protocol mode.
4. Add scout packet + submit_area_dossier flow with scout read-only tool restrictions.
5. Add builder packet + submit_completion_report flow with mutation blocked until valid packet exists.
6. Add dashboard visibility for protocol artifacts.

Relationship to other work:
- Should follow the Docker/devcontainer migration because role-specific spawning and filesystem/state sharing will be simpler and safer once the bwrap-specific assumptions are removed.
- Complements Mulch and Seeds: Seeds tracks work, Mulch stores curated durable knowledge, protocol artifacts provide operational handoff/context and may generate candidates for Mulch promotion.
closeReason: Closed/deferred to simplify active roadmap. Packet-gated Protocol Mode is superseded for now by pi-agent-orchestrator-0403 Workflow/WorkflowRun design; strict protocol concepts can be reintroduced later as workflow policy/profile constraints.

title: Explore agent retro surveys for capability discovery
description:
Brainstorm/prototype standardized end-of-task surveys for spawned agents to identify missing tools, skills, extensions, or workflow affordances.

Idea:
- At the end of each sub-agent task, ask the agent what would have made the task easier, faster, safer, or more reliable.
- Collect responses in a standard structured format across roles (lead/scout/builder/reviewer).
- Over time, cluster similar requests to identify recurring capability gaps.
- Use findings to decide which features, skills, extensions, or agent-type defaults to add.

Potential fit:
- Hook into Orchestration Protocol Mode completion artifacts:
  - Scout survey after Area Dossier submission.
  - Builder survey after Completion Report submission.
  - Lead survey after final synthesis.
- Treat survey as advisory telemetry, not authoritative backlog input.
- Store role, task type, issue id, packet id, tools available, tools used, blocked actions, pain points, requested capability, expected impact.
- Embeddings/vector similarity may be useful for clustering semantically similar requests over time.

Important design cautions:
- Ask behavior-focused questions: "What slowed you down?" / "What information or tool was missing?" rather than only "What feature do you want?"
- Require concrete evidence/context where possible.
- De-duplicate and human-review before promoting to roadmap.
- Avoid making surveys mandatory enough to block task completion unless protocol mode intentionally requires a final self-assessment field.

Example survey fields:
- role
- issueId / packetId
- taskSummary
- missingContext
- missingToolOrFunctionality
- workaroundUsed
- expectedTimeSavings
- confidence
- suggestedCapabilityType: skill | tool | extension | atlas-context | protocol-change | docs | unknown
- evidenceRefs

Acceptance for exploration:
- Decide whether surveys belong inside protocol artifacts or as separate telemetry artifacts.
- Define minimal schema.
- Prototype collection for at least one role.
- Sketch clustering/reporting approach, optionally with embeddings.

title: Add context-budget handoff protocol for long-running agents
description:
Brainstormed future capability: keep all spawned agents operating in a healthy context range, ideally under ~60% of max context, by detecting high context usage and triggering a structured handoff.

Motivation:
- Models appear to degrade once context climbs past roughly 60-70%.
- Long-running leads/builders/scouts should not keep accumulating context until quality drops.
- Protocol Mode already plans packet-gated handoffs; context-budget handoff should build on those artifacts rather than inventing a separate flow.

Concept:
- Orchestration engine monitors context budget/token usage per agent.
- Warning threshold around 60%; mandatory handoff threshold around 70% unless explicitly overridden.
- When threshold is reached, orchestrator sends a cleanup/handoff sequence to the agent.
- Agent produces a structured handoff report: current objective, completed work, files read/changed, validation state, pending steps, risks, exact next actions, and any packet/report artifacts that should be carried forward.
- Orchestrator updates the corresponding Seeds issue with current state or links/stores the handoff artifact.
- Orchestrator terminates or retires the saturated agent and spawns a fresh agent under the appropriate parent with a compact continuation packet.

Lead-specific guidance:
- Leads should rarely need lead-to-lead handoff; if a lead crosses the budget, that may indicate the issue was too broad or the Issue Context Packet/orientation packet was too weak.
- Lead handoff can exist as a safety valve, but should emit a diagnostic suggesting issue slicing or packet improvements.

Potential protocol artifacts:
- ContextBudgetStatus
- HandoffRequest
- AgentHandoffReport
- ContinuationPacket

Acceptance criteria for eventual implementation:
- Define budget thresholds and override behavior.
- Add context-budget monitoring to orchestration runtime/dashboard.
- Add protocol schema/store support for handoff artifacts.
- Add agent steering prompt for cleanup/handoff.
- Update Seeds issue state or comments/description with resumable handoff summary.
- Spawn replacement agent with only necessary continuation context and relevant protocol artifacts.
- Add tests for threshold detection and handoff artifact validation.

title: Develop orchestrator issue-slicing pushback skill/protocol
description:
Build explicit orchestrator/lead behavior for pushing back on overly broad coding requests and splitting work into narrow Seeds issues before execution.

Motivation:
- Users may ask for work that sounds like one task but is actually complex/substantial.
- The root orchestrator is expected to run the strongest/deepest-reasoning model and should protect execution quality by enforcing good coding practice: small, testable, narrowly scoped issues.
- Leads and scouts may discover additional complexity after deeper repo investigation and should be able to recommend splitting the issue further.
- Context-budget handoff diagnostics should feed back into this: if a lead/agent needs handoff due to context saturation, the issue or packet may have been too broad.

Core behavior:
- Orchestrator challenges vague or oversized requests before spawning agents.
- Orchestrator can say: this is not a small issue; we should break it into N tracer bullets/sub-issues.
- Orchestrator has final authority to create/split/sequence Seeds issues.
- Lead can submit an Issue Split Recommendation after investigation, including rationale, proposed sub-issues, dependencies, and whether current work should pause.
- Orchestrator reviews the recommendation and either accepts, modifies, rejects, or asks for more evidence.

Potential skill/protocol pieces:
- A project skill for issue sizing and pushback, applied to root orchestrator and optionally leads.
- Sizing rubric: number of files/areas, unknowns, expected validation burden, schema/API changes, UI+backend coupling, risk, required research/scouting, and context budget estimate.
- Protocol artifact: IssueSplitRecommendation from lead -> orchestrator.
- Protocol artifact: IssueSlicingDecision from orchestrator -> lead/root state.
- Seeds integration: create child/sub-issues with dependencies; update original issue as epic/parent/context; close/supersede or keep as tracking epic where appropriate.

Acceptance criteria:
- Define issue-sizing rubric and examples of acceptable pushback language.
- Implement or scaffold an orchestrator skill that teaches narrow issue enforcement.
- Add protocol artifact schema for lead IssueSplitRecommendation.
- Add root/orchestrator flow for accepting a split and creating/updating Seeds issues.
- Ensure lead recommendations do not directly mutate Seeds without orchestrator approval.
- Add dashboard or log visibility for split recommendations/decisions.
- Document that lead-to-lead handoff or repeated context-budget pressure is a signal to re-slice work.

title: Epic: Read-only Roadmap dashboard backed by Seeds
description:
Add a read-only Roadmap dashboard view so users can see project status, epics, issue hierarchy, blockers/dependencies, next-up work, and closed/completed Seeds without asking the agent to query Seeds manually.

Product direction:
- Name the dashboard feature Roadmap, not Seeds, because Seeds is the current backing store but may later be replaced by a SQLite roadmap/task store.
- Keep the first implementation read-only. Do not add dashboard controls that mutate Seeds.
- Implement as a clean vertical feature module under web/features/roadmap.
- Use Seeds internally for now, but expose dashboard-facing Roadmap types/DTOs so the UI is not tightly named around Seeds internals.

Desired v1 UX:
- A Roadmap tab in the dashboard.
- Summary counts for in-progress, ready/next-up, blocked, backlog/open, and closed work.
- Epic-oriented hierarchy where possible, with ungrouped issues separately visible.
- Dependency/blocker badges that make it clear why an issue is blocked and what it blocks.
- A next-up/ready queue sorted by priority and project conventions.
- A read-only detail panel/drawer for selected issues.

Epic acceptance:
- Dashboard has a Roadmap tab implemented through web/features/roadmap.
- Backend provides a read-only roadmap overview derived from current Seeds data.
- UI clearly distinguishes in-progress, ready, blocked, backlog/open, and closed issues.
- UI shows epic/dependency relationships enough to discuss project state from the dashboard.
- The feature is structured so a future SQLite-backed roadmap store can replace Seeds behind the API without renaming the UI feature.
- bun run check passes.

title: Future: web/network guardrails for containerized agents
description:
Deferred hardening follow-up for the containerized orchestration runtime epic.

Context:
- Docker/process mode can constrain filesystem writes with per-agent worktrees and read-only vs read-write mounts, but network access remains a separate exfiltration/download risk.
- pi-web-access currently exposes internet-facing tools such as `web_search`, `fetch_content`, `code_search`, and `get_search_content` without an orchestration-specific policy layer.
- Pi extensions can intercept `tool_call`, so the likely first implementation should be a companion guardrail/broker extension rather than an immediate fork of pi-web-access.

Desired future design:
- Add role-based web/network policy for spawned agents.
- Intercept web-access tool calls and inspect queries/URLs before execution.
- Deny localhost/private IPs, metadata endpoints, `file://` URLs, suspicious local paths, and obvious secret/code exfiltration patterns.
- Allow common documentation/research domains according to role policy.
- Add audit logging for agent name, tool, query/url, allow/deny result, and reason.
- Pair tool-level policy with container network enforcement so agents cannot bypass web-access using raw `bash` commands like `curl`, `wget`, `node fetch`, or Python requests.
- Keep local bash computation available for token-efficient analysis (`rg`, `find`, local scripts), while controlling network egress and risky download/execute flows.

Risks/examples to handle:
- `curl | sh`, `wget | bash`, arbitrary package installs with lifecycle scripts, downloading unknown binaries, or exfiltrating repo/secrets via HTTP.
- GitHub/documentation fetching should be allowed where appropriate, but cloned/fetched code should not be executed without separate approval/policy.

Priority note:
- P4/deferred. Do not block the current Docker/process migration, capability allowlist fixes, mounted library discovery, or handoff artifact skeleton work.

title: Epic: Dashboard reactive resource refresh and live state overlays
description:
Make the Pi Lattice dashboard progressively dynamic so tabs update from backend/resource changes without requiring manual Refresh.

Goal:
- Move from each tab independently fetching on mount/manual click to a small app-wide reactive resource system.
- Keep the implementation lightweight: use the existing SSE `/events` push channel, resource invalidation events, normal HTTP refetches, and tab-local view state preservation.
- Prefer small tracer bullets over introducing a large client state framework prematurely.

Core principle: push invalidations, pull data.
- Server pushes lightweight change notifications such as `resource-changed`.
- Client marks affected resources stale.
- Active tabs refetch quietly; inactive tabs show stale indicators and refetch when opened.
- SSE should not carry large Roadmap/Skill/Template payloads unless a future use case requires it.

Proposed layers:
1. Event / push layer
   - Reuse the existing dashboard EventSource connection.
   - Add semantic events like `{ type: "resource-changed", data: { resource, reason, version } }`.
   - Resources may include `roadmap`, `skills`, `templates`, `agent-types`, `libraries`, `root-profiles`, and `live-agents`.

2. Server change detection layer
   - Add a central helper such as `broadcastResourceChanged(resource, reason)`.
   - Dashboard mutation endpoints call it after changing libraries/templates/skills/agent types.
   - File watchers or debounced polling notice external changes, especially `.seeds/issues.jsonl` for Roadmap updates caused by Seeds CLI, agents, or other processes.
   - One backend action may invalidate multiple resources, e.g. enabling a library affects libraries, skills, templates, agent-types, and maybe root profiles.

3. Client invalidation layer
   - `web/app.tsx` owns the SSE connection and resource invalidation registry.
   - Track resource versions/stale flags/reasons/timestamps.
   - Pass relevant invalidation state into feature panels rather than each panel opening its own EventSource.

4. Fetch / refresh layer
   - Feature panels refetch when their resource is stale and the tab is active.
   - Inactive panels can remain stale and fetch on activation.
   - Manual Refresh remains as an escape hatch.
   - Quiet refresh keeps existing UI visible and optionally shows an Updating indicator instead of replacing content with a loading screen.

5. View state preservation layer
   - Refetches should update data without unnecessarily collapsing expanded sections or closing dialogs.
   - Keep UI state keyed by stable IDs, such as selected issue id and expanded epic ids.
   - If selected data disappears, show a graceful stale/missing state.

6. Runtime overlay layer
   - Durable project state remains in Seeds/Mulch/resource files.
   - Live runtime state such as active agents, roles, worktrees, artifact paths, disposable test sessions, and runtime tools should overlay on top of persisted views.
   - Roadmap eventually should show active agents working on an issue without forcing all runtime details into Seeds.

Initial Roadmap target:
- Watch or debounce-detect `.seeds/issues.jsonl` changes.
- Broadcast `resource-changed: roadmap`.
- RoadmapPanel quietly refetches `/api/roadmap` when active, preserving expanded epic/detail modal state.
- Inactive Roadmap tab shows stale state and refreshes on activation.

Future expansion:
- Apply the same invalidation pattern to Lattice Libraries, Skill Library, Templates, Agent Types, Extensions, Root Profiles, and runtime/test-session visibility.
- Add issue-agent association metadata so Roadmap can show live Lead/Scout/Builder/Reviewer activity per issue.
- Add stale badges in tab labels.
- Add tests for server broadcasts, client invalidation, quiet refetch, and state preservation.

Relationship to existing work:
- Complements the read-only Roadmap provider model: Roadmap remains backed by `/api/roadmap` and Seeds-derived DTOs.
- Complements the runtime/handoff epic: live issue-agent overlays can later use issue ids, worktree paths, and artifact paths from the orchestration runtime.
- Does not replace manual refresh immediately; it adds dynamic behavior incrementally.
closeReason: Closed/deferred to simplify active roadmap. Generic dashboard reactivity can be revisited later; v1 workflow runtime visibility now lives under pi-agent-orchestrator-0403, especially the Live Agents WorkflowRun overlay tracer.

title: Epic: Full spawned-agent observability and transcripts
description:
Build first-class observability for every spawned agent so the root orchestrator/user can see what happened from birth through each turn, without guessing from stale idle cards or raw logs.

Motivation:
- Test handoff to lead-15e6 showed the prompt was written to stdin, but the dashboard showed only idle/silent state until the user killed the agent.
- Dashboard sends currently show queued status but do not display the sent user prompt, preflight/progress state, stderr, or pending timeout.
- For orchestration to be trustworthy, each spawned Lead/Scout/Builder/Reviewer must have an inspectable lifecycle and transcript: what it was launched with, what it saw, what messages it received, what events/tools it emitted, what errors occurred, and why it stopped.

Desired operator experience:
- From the dashboard, open any spawned agent and see a chronological timeline from spawn.
- Timeline includes spawn command metadata, system prompt/agent definition references, initial handoff, every user/send/steer message, model turn events, assistant deltas/final text, tool starts/ends, stderr/preflight errors, timeouts, exits, kills, and worktree/artifact paths.
- Agent cards distinguish queued, writing, preflight/waiting, streaming, idle, errored, exited, and killed states where practical.
- Inspect can fetch detailed context/transcript on demand without dumping all child context into the root orchestrator.
- Long transcripts should be durable enough for debugging a session after dashboard refresh/reconnect, with sensible redaction/size limits.

Initial scope:
- Improve observability of the current bwrap/RPC prototype enough to diagnose blocked agents.
- Keep implementation compatible with the planned Docker/direct-process migration; avoid over-investing in bwrap-specific UI.
- Store operational transcript/debug artifacts under the app-owned `.pi/pi-agent-orchestrator/` tree when persistence is needed.

Out of scope for this epic:
- Full packet-gated orchestration protocol enforcement.
- Automatic issue handoff artifact semantics beyond raw/transcript observability.
- Model-provider-specific context-window introspection unless already available via existing stats/state APIs.

Implement a provider-shaped Roadmap mutation API:

- Return a refreshed Roadmap DTO (preferably full `RoadmapOverview`) so the dashboard can update from the same provider-shaped contract.
- Keep Seeds-specific command details inside `extensions/multi-agent/roadmap.ts` or a nearby server-side helper; the web UI should not know about Seeds.

title: Design visual workflow orchestration for Pi Lattice
description:
Brainstorm and design a first-class workflow layer for Pi Lattice that lets users define, visualize, validate, and run multi-agent orchestration patterns beyond prompt-only root profiles.

Context:
- Today `/orchestrate` activates a root profile and gives the root model orchestration tools, but the actual process is advisory/prompt-defined.
- We want explicit workflows that can define phases, agent topology, role/profile choices, handoff rules, spawn permissions, review gates, loops, validation rules, and app/project-specific variants.
- Users should be able to build and inspect workflows in the dashboard through a lightweight YAML/text-first interface with a generated Mermaid preview, avoiding heavy canvas dependencies for v1.
- AI-assisted workflow authoring should allow full YAML replacement, but only through validation + automatic repair loops + user approval before persistence.

Design direction captured:
- Add Workflow as a first-class Lattice Library resource, versioned and user-owned.
- Authored Workflow source is YAML only. JSON may exist internally as DTO/API shape, but not as a persisted authoring format.
- Mermaid is generated confirmation/preview only, never source of truth.
- Dashboard workflow editor should be YAML-first with outline/details/preview/validation areas rather than a complex drag-and-drop canvas or full schema form editor.
- Static workflow blueprint Mermaid should show optional/dynamic child-spawn possibilities as visually distinct, e.g. dashed outlines/edges, because those children may or may not be spawned at runtime.
- Live Agents/runtime dashboard should separately show the actual WorkflowRun topology that is running now, potentially as a runtime Mermaid overlay.
- Workflow edges represent workflow control-flow transitions only. Spawn/message/handoff semantics should not be overloaded into edges.
- Child spawning is represented as a node capability/policy: which direct child agent types this node/agent may spawn.
- Child results are represented as parent/child handoff requirements: expected summary fields, artifact refs, completion criteria, and failure/escalation behavior.
- Loops/cycles are allowed for review/rework/refinement, but validation should require an exit condition, max iterations, timeout, or escalation path.
- Runtime state ownership should move to an extension-owned WorkflowRun control plane, not the root model context.
- WorkflowRun owns live topology, active nodes, spawned agent metadata, parent/child relationships, handoff metadata, audit events, loop counts, gate state, and validation/guardrail enforcement.
- Parent means the live agent instance that requested/spawned the child through WorkflowRun tools.
- Agents may spawn direct child agents through workflow-authorized tools; spawning and messaging are audited by the runtime.
- V1 communication model is intentionally simple: bidirectional immediate parent ↔ direct-child communication only. No arbitrary peer-to-peer routing and no grandchild-to-grandparent routing.
- Multi-depth spawning may exist conceptually, but communication never skips levels; the first implementation may cap depth at 2 if needed to reduce complexity.
- Root/orchestrator starts/supervises/synthesizes a WorkflowRun, but should not receive every child transcript or raw log by default.
- Root/orchestrator reads directly from the top-level agents it spawned, usually a lead or scout, and synthesizes from those summaries/handoffs.
- Short-lived agents such as scouts should be able to summarize findings to their parent and exit without flooding unrelated/root context.
- User-to-agent steering can remain in the Live Agents panel for v1; special `@agent` prompt routing is deferred.
- AI Assist should let the user pick an accessible Pi model, inject a static workflow-authoring skill/spec plus dynamic schema/context bundle, request a full replacement YAML, validate it, automatically repair on errors, then show diff + Mermaid preview for approval.
- Runtime integration should distinguish reusable Workflow definitions from live WorkflowRun state.

Core design questions:
- What minimal v1 node types are required, and which proposed types should be aliases/subtypes/deferred?
- What is the exact Workflow YAML schema and validation contract?
- How do workflows relate to root profiles, agent definitions, skills, extension templates, and tool capability policies without duplicating too much policy?
- What minimal workflow fields express parent-authorized child spawning and parent/child handoff requirements without becoming a complex communication graph?
- What exact runtime tools let a parent agent create/direct a child and receive a child summary/handoff through WorkflowRun-owned state?
- How are child summaries/handoffs stored, surfaced to the parent, and kept out of unrelated/root context?
- How do Live Agents direct messages, runtime topology, generated runtime Mermaid, and dashboard timeline interact with active WorkflowRun agents?

Tracer bullets under this epic:
Foundation/schema/resource:
- pi-agent-orchestrator-6124 — Add Lattice Library Workflow resource discovery and persistence.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-7fda — Integrate Workflow validation status with resource APIs.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-5d69 — Define core Workflow YAML schema v1 node types.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-cece — Define Workflow validation error schema.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-6bea — Define Workflow schema with validation and loop semantics.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-937c — Define Workflow Mermaid generation for blueprint and runtime views.

Dashboard/editor:
- pi-agent-orchestrator-ebc9 — Build text-first workflow editor with live Mermaid preview.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-f4b1 — Add Live Agents WorkflowRun runtime overlay.

AI-assisted authoring:
- pi-agent-orchestrator-0e4e — Create workflow-authoring skill/spec for AI assistance.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-a2c0 — Design model selection and context bundling for workflow AI assist.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-08f2 — Design automatic workflow validation repair loop for AI authoring.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-ce6d — Prototype AI-assisted workflow authoring.

- pi-agent-orchestrator-5022 — Define WorkflowRun parent-child runtime contract.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-ba71 — Define WorkflowRun state model.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-c3b8 — Define parent-child WorkflowRun spawn tool semantics.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-f7ab — Define parent-child WorkflowRun message and handoff tool semantics.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-30c0 — Define WorkflowRun event/audit log.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-00a0 — Define WorkflowRun failure timeout and escalation behavior.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-8dad — Integrate Workflow runtime execution with orchestrator tools.

- pi-agent-orchestrator-c03b — Prompt-level streaming/@agent routing; v1 uses Live Agents direct messaging instead.

Initial epic acceptance:

- Decide minimal v1 node types and source-of-truth/Mermaid generation rules.
- Sketch dashboard information architecture for workflow gallery + YAML editor + outline + Mermaid preview + validation panel.
- Define validation errors suitable for both user display and AI repair.
- Define AI-assisted authoring flow with model selection, static workflow-authoring skill injection, dynamic context bundle, full YAML replacement, validation loop, diff, preview, and approval.
- Define WorkflowRun runtime state and relationship to root, parent agents, direct child agents, handoffs, loops, direct Live Agents messages, and runtime overlays.
- Keep v1 runtime communication simple: immediate parent ↔ direct-child only, with all spawning/messaging mediated by audited WorkflowRun tools.

title: Prototype AI-assisted workflow authoring
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403.

Prototype an AI-assisted workflow authoring flow for Pi Lattice. Users should be able to describe their desired multi-agent workflow in natural language, select an accessible model to assist, and receive a validated full replacement Workflow YAML plus regenerated Mermaid preview.

Decisions captured:
- AI assist returns full replacement YAML for v1, not patches/JSON Patch.
- Full YAML replacement is acceptable because validation and automatic repair protect correctness.
- Assistant should not directly persist files; dashboard/extension applies only validated, approved YAML.
- Workflow-authoring guidance starts as a dedicated SKILL.md injected into the model through the authoring API.

Flow:
1. User opens AI Assist Workflow.
2. User selects assistant model from existing Pi model discovery.
3. User describes desired workflow changes.
4. Assistant receives workflow-authoring skill, current workflow YAML, generated Mermaid, available refs, validation constraints, and instruction.
5. Assistant returns complete replacement YAML.
6. Dashboard/extension validates and repairs if needed.
7. User sees diff + Mermaid preview + validation summary.
8. Save only after user approval.

Acceptance:
- Define workflow-authoring skill injection strategy.
- Define context bundle.
- Define full YAML replacement output contract.
- Validate proposal and show diff + Mermaid preview before applying.
- Persist accepted workflow only inside writable Lattice Library workflow resources.

title: Define Workflow schema with validation and loop semantics
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403.

Define the first Pi Lattice Workflow YAML schema, validation rules, loop semantics, and minimal parent/child runtime semantics.

Decisions captured:
- YAML is the only authored source-of-truth format.
- JSON may exist as internal DTO/API shape, but users author and persist Workflow YAML.
- Workflow node types for v1 are decided in pi-agent-orchestrator-5d69 before broad schema expansion.
- Workflows may contain cycles/loops, but only bounded cycles are valid.
- Every intentional cycle must have an exit/limit/escalation mechanism such as `until`, `maxIterations`, `timeout`, `onExhausted`, or an explicit valid exit edge.
- Mermaid is generated from validated Workflow YAML and is not source of truth; detailed Mermaid rules are split to pi-agent-orchestrator-937c.
- Runtime state is owned by WorkflowRun, not by root model context.
- Parent means the live agent instance that requested/spawned the child through WorkflowRun tools.
- V1 communication is bidirectional immediate parent ↔ direct-child only.
- Multi-depth spawning may exist conceptually, but communication never skips levels; first implementation may cap depth at 2.
- Parent agents may spawn only workflow-authorized direct child agent types.
- Child agents return structured summaries/handoff packets to their parent so raw logs do not flood unrelated/root context.

Schema areas:
- workflow metadata: name, description, version, library namespace, tags/application type
- minimal v1 node types and required fields from pi-agent-orchestrator-5d69
- edges/transitions as workflow control-flow only, not spawn/message/handoff overloads
- agent bindings: agent type/profile, model preference, skills, extension templates, tool allow/exclude policy
- simple child spawn policy: whether this node can spawn direct children and which child agent types are allowed
- parent/child handoff requirements: expected child summary fields, artifact refs, completion criteria, failure/escalation behavior
- entry/exit criteria and handoff packet requirements
- loop controls: maxIterations, until condition, timeout, escalation/fallback node

Acceptance:
- Draft schema and examples after v1 node types are decided.
- Include simple feature workflow, review loop, three-stage review, parent-spawns-scout workflow, and human approval/gate example.
- Use structured validation errors from pi-agent-orchestrator-cece.
- Define validation for direct-child spawn policies and parent/child handoff requirements.
- Explicitly avoid v1 validation complexity for arbitrary multi-agent communication graphs.

title: Design automatic workflow validation repair loop for AI authoring
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403 and related to pi-agent-orchestrator-ce6d.

Design/prototype an automatic validation feedback loop for AI-assisted Workflow YAML authoring.

Decisions captured:
- Dashboard/API extension owns the validation and repair loop.
- Do not rely on the user to notice validation errors.
- Do not rely on a freeform model hook as the primary validation mechanism.
- Retry limit is 3 automatic repair attempts.
- If still invalid after retries, show the last YAML and structured errors to the user.
- Validation must understand the revised v1 runtime model: WorkflowRun-owned state plus simple parent ↔ direct-child communication only.
- Validation should reject or warn on unnecessary arbitrary communication graphs, peer-to-peer routes, or grandchild-to-grandparent routing in v1 workflows.

Desired flow:
1. User opens AI Assist Workflow and selects an accessible model.
2. User describes desired workflow changes.
3. Assistant receives current workflow YAML, workflow-authoring SKILL.md/spec, available refs, and user instruction.
4. Assistant returns full replacement YAML.
5. Validation script/tool runs automatically.
6. If validation fails, feed structured validation errors plus invalid YAML back to the assistant for repair.
7. Repeat up to 3 repair attempts.
8. Show final YAML diff + Mermaid preview + validation summary to user for approval if valid.
9. Save only after approval.

Validation areas:
- Schema shape and required metadata.
- Referenced nodes, agent types, skills, extension templates, and tool policies.
- Bounded loop semantics.
- Direct-child spawn policies: parent node may only spawn allowed direct child agent types.
- Parent/child handoff requirements: child must have a valid summary/completion path back to parent.
- V1 scope guardrails: no arbitrary peer-to-peer, grandchild-to-grandparent, or full communication graph requirements.

Acceptance:
- Define extension/dashboard API loop owner.
- Define validation output schema optimized for model repair.
- Implement retry policy: max 3 repairs.
- Define failure UX after retry exhaustion.
- Prototype with full YAML replacement and automatic repair on validation errors.
- Include validation checks for simple parent-child runtime semantics without overcomplicating the schema.

title: Add Lattice Library Workflow resource discovery and persistence
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403.

Add Workflows as first-class Lattice Library resources so teams can version, share, and select orchestration blueprints alongside agents, root profiles, skills, extension templates, and prompts.

Decisions captured:
- Workflows live under Lattice Libraries.
- Standard resource directory should be `workflows/`.
- Authored Workflow source is YAML only.
- If multiple writable Lattice Libraries are available, the user chooses which library to save a new workflow under.
- Saving accepted edits must stay inside a writable enabled Lattice Library workflow directory.
- Full schema validation/API status is split to pi-agent-orchestrator-7fda; this tracer should establish safe discovery and persistence first.

Acceptance:
- Define manifest/resource path convention for `resources.workflows: "workflows"`.
- Discover workflow YAML files with source library, read-only/editable status, and namespace.
- Add API shape for listing, reading, creating, and updating workflow resources with path guards.
- Preserve the Lattice Library path guard model: no arbitrary file writes outside configured workflow resource directories.
- Return lightweight parse/read diagnostics where practical, but do not make full validation integration part of this tracer.

title: Build text-first workflow editor with live Mermaid preview
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403.

Prototype the lightweight dashboard workflow editor: a YAML-first interface that generates a Mermaid preview in real time. Avoid heavy drag-and-drop canvas dependencies and avoid a full schema form editor for v1.

Decisions captured:
- Workflow YAML is source of truth.
- Mermaid preview is generated only.
- V1 editor should be YAML/text-first with validation, preview, and outline/navigation support.
- Page layout should be split horizontally:
  - top section: workflow YAML editor, outline, and selected block summary
  - bottom section: Mermaid preview and validation output
- UI should use lightweight controls rather than a complex canvas.
- Do not expose a complex communication-permission graph for v1.
- If spawn policy appears in the editor, keep it simple: which direct child agent types this parent node may spawn, plus required child summary/handoff fields.

Acceptance:
- Sketch dashboard IA and component boundaries around the horizontal split.
- Edit Workflow YAML and parse/validate live.
- Generate static blueprint Mermaid from a minimal valid workflow.
- Show optional/dynamic child-spawn possibilities using the Mermaid conventions from pi-agent-orchestrator-937c.
- Show validation status before save.
- Provide outline/selected-node summary without requiring complete form editing for every schema field.
- Keep v1 communication/spawn editing intentionally simple: parent ↔ direct-child only.

title: Create workflow-authoring skill/spec for AI assistance
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403. Supports pi-agent-orchestrator-ce6d and pi-agent-orchestrator-08f2.

Create a dedicated workflow-authoring SKILL.md/spec that can be injected into AI-assisted workflow authoring sessions.

Decisions captured:
- Start with a SKILL.md file for v1.
- The authoring API injects this skill/spec into the selected model.
- The static skill should cover principles and authoring behavior, not embed dynamic/user-modifiable schema details.
- Current schema, available refs, examples, and validation errors are injected separately in the context bundle.
- The skill must teach the revised runtime model: WorkflowRun is the durable control plane, root is not the universal message relay, and direct agent communication is limited to immediate parent ↔ direct-child in v1.
- The skill should discourage overcomplicated arbitrary communication graphs.

Static skill/spec should cover:
- Workflow resource purpose and source-of-truth rules.
- YAML-only authoring rule.
- WorkflowRun-owned runtime state and audit/guardrail expectations.
- General agent binding/reference principles.
- Capability safety expectations.
- Simple parent-authorized child spawning principle.
- Simple communication principle: parent can message child, child can summarize/handoff back to parent; no peer/grandparent routing for v1.
- Loop/cycle principles: allowed only when bounded by exit condition, max iterations, timeout, or escalation.
- Mermaid is generated preview only.
- Full YAML replacement output contract.
- Validation repair behavior: consume structured errors and return corrected complete YAML.

Acceptance:
- Draft SKILL.md content.
- Include examples or guidance that avoid unnecessary complex communication permissions.
- Define exact assistant response format for full YAML replacement.
- Define which contextual references are injected separately from the static skill.

title: Integrate Workflow runtime execution with orchestrator tools
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403.

Design/prototype how an active Workflow definition influences runtime orchestration after `/orchestrate` and task start.

Revised decisions captured:
- Runtime v1 should use an extension-owned WorkflowRun control plane as the durable owner of live workflow state, not the root model context.
- Root starts/supervises/synthesizes a WorkflowRun, but should not be the universal relay for child transcripts or raw logs.
- Root/orchestrator reads directly from the top-level agents it spawned, usually a lead or scout, and synthesizes from those summaries/handoffs.
- WorkflowRun state tracks execution separately from reusable Workflow definitions.
- Parent means the live agent instance that requested/spawned the child through WorkflowRun tools.
- Agents may spawn direct child agents through WorkflowRun-authorized tools; the runtime enforces guardrails, records audit events, and owns metadata writes.
- Communication is intentionally limited to bidirectional immediate parent ↔ direct-child communication.
- Multi-depth spawning can exist conceptually, but communication never skips levels; first implementation may cap depth at 2.
- No arbitrary peer-to-peer routing or grandchild-to-grandparent routing is required for v1.
- Short-lived child agents such as scouts can summarize findings to their parent and exit, keeping unrelated/root context clean.
- Dashboard/Live Agents observability remains first-class so direct agent work is inspectable without injecting all output into root context.

Runtime breakdown:
- pi-agent-orchestrator-5022 — Define WorkflowRun parent-child runtime contract.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-ba71 — Define WorkflowRun state model.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-c3b8 — Define parent-child WorkflowRun spawn tool semantics.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-f7ab — Define parent-child WorkflowRun message and handoff tool semantics.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-30c0 — Define WorkflowRun event/audit log.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-00a0 — Define WorkflowRun failure timeout and escalation behavior.
- pi-agent-orchestrator-f4b1 — Add Live Agents WorkflowRun runtime overlay.

Acceptance:
- Integrate the runtime contract, state model, spawn/message/handoff tools, event log, failure semantics, and dashboard overlay.
- Define how workflow selection is activated from `/orchestrate` or dashboard.
- Preserve audited runtime-owned state and guardrails while allowing direct parent-child agent communication.
- Prevent v1 scope creep into arbitrary communication graphs.
- Identify minimal tracer implementation path.

title: Map streaming steers and follow-ups into active Workflow runs
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403.

Design how Pi's streaming-aware input events (`streamingBehavior`: steer | followUp | idle/undefined) should interact with active Workflow runs and dashboard timeline.

Revised decisions captured:
- For v1, user-to-agent direct messaging can happen through the Live Agents panel, where users can see running agents and message them individually.
- Special `@agent` prompt routing syntax is not required for the first workflow runtime tracer.
- During active workflow runs, Live Agents messages to workflow-owned agents should still be recorded as WorkflowRun/user intervention events.
- Queued follow-up input can remain normal Pi/root behavior unless the dashboard explicitly targets a workflow agent or node.
- The runtime should avoid expensive preprocessing for low-latency user interventions.

Examples for v1 dashboard messaging:
- User opens Live Agents panel, selects `scout-1`, sends: `stop searching node_modules`.
- User selects `reviewer`, sends: `after this check security implications`.
- User selects `lead`, sends: `summarize the implementation risks`.

Design areas:
- Mapping dashboard-selected agent messages to active spawned agent and WorkflowRun intervention events.
- Timeline/insight events: idle prompt, dashboard agent message, follow-up, extension-injected message, child result/handoff.
- Latency policy: skip expensive preprocessing for direct agent messages; enrich idle/follow-up messages when useful.
- Dashboard runtime overlay: show user interventions on graph nodes and identify agents requiring frequent steering.

Acceptance:
- Define input routing rules for Live Agents direct messages during active workflow runs.
- Define telemetry event schema for dashboard agent messages, follow-ups, and idle inputs.
- Decide when follow-ups become WorkflowRun queued actions versus normal Pi queued messages.
- Sketch dashboard display for interventions on the workflow graph/timeline.
- Defer `@agent` prompt syntax unless later user testing shows it is needed.
closeReason: Deferred from v1 workflow runtime scope. Prompt-level streaming/@agent routing is not needed initially; v1 user-to-agent intervention will happen through Live Agents direct messaging and WorkflowRun runtime overlay work in pi-agent-orchestrator-f4b1.

title: Design model selection and context bundling for workflow AI assist
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403. Supports pi-agent-orchestrator-ce6d.

Design the model-selection and context-bundling layer for AI-assisted workflow authoring.

Decisions captured:
- Reuse existing Pi model discovery/accessibility logic for model choices.
- Default can be the current root model, but user can select another accessible model.
- Context should be bounded summaries/structured refs rather than arbitrary full filesystem access.
- Assistant cannot directly persist files; dashboard/extension applies only validated approved YAML.
- Static workflow-authoring skill contains principles; current schema, available refs, examples, and validation errors are injected dynamically.
- Safest v1 execution model is an extension-owned model call or tightly restricted temporary session with no write tools; final design still needs selection.

Context bundle:
- Current workflow YAML.
- Workflow schema version and current schema reference.
- Generated Mermaid preview.
- Workflow-authoring SKILL.md/spec.
- Available root profiles.
- Available agent definitions and classes.
- Available skills and extension templates.
- Tool names / capability policies / known excluded-tool presets.
- Validation constraints and current structured validation errors, if repairing.
- Runtime constraints: WorkflowRun-owned state, parent definition, immediate parent-child communication only, possible v1 depth cap.
- User natural-language instruction.

Acceptance:
- Define API shape for model list used by workflow assist.
- Define context bundle shape and size limits.
- Decide whether assistant runs in a temporary Pi session, SDK session, or extension-owned model call.
- Ensure assistant has no direct save/write authority for workflow resources.

title: Define parent-child WorkflowRun spawn tool semantics
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403.

Define/prototype the WorkflowRun-authorized tool/API that lets a live parent agent spawn a direct child agent.

title: Define WorkflowRun state model
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403.

Define the live WorkflowRun state shape used by runtime tools, dashboard APIs, and generated runtime Mermaid overlays.

Acceptance:
- Define WorkflowRun identifiers, selected Workflow ref/version, root/top-level agent refs, active/completed nodes, spawned agent instances, parent-child links, loop counters, gate state, pending handoffs, failures, and timestamps.

title: Define WorkflowRun parent-child runtime contract
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403.

Define the minimal v1 runtime contract for WorkflowRun-owned parent/child agent execution.

Decisions captured:
- WorkflowRun is the durable runtime/control-plane owner.
- Parent means the live agent instance that requested/spawned the child through WorkflowRun tools.
- Communication is bidirectional only between immediate parent and direct child.
- Multi-depth spawning may exist conceptually, but communication never skips levels. For the first implementation, depth may be capped at 2 if needed to reduce complexity.
- Root/orchestrator reads directly from top-level agents it spawned, usually a lead, and synthesizes from those summaries rather than raw child logs.

Acceptance:

- Define handoff packet expectations between child and parent.

title: Define parent-child WorkflowRun message and handoff tool semantics
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403.

Define/prototype the WorkflowRun-authorized tool/API for parent-to-child messages and child-to-parent summaries/handoffs.

Acceptance:

title: Define Workflow Mermaid generation for blueprint and runtime views
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403.

Define Mermaid generation rules for both static Workflow blueprint previews and live WorkflowRun runtime overlays.

Decisions captured:

Acceptance:

title: Define Workflow validation error schema
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403.

Define the structured validation error format used by Workflow resource validation, dashboard validation panels, AI repair loops, and save/update APIs.

Decisions captured:
- Validation errors must be model-friendly and user-displayable.
- Errors should include stable code, severity, path, message, repair hint, and optional related refs.

title: Define WorkflowRun event/audit log
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403.

Define the WorkflowRun event/audit log used for runtime observability, dashboard timelines, debugging, and guardrail verification.

Acceptance:

title: Integrate Workflow validation status with resource APIs
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403. Split from pi-agent-orchestrator-6124.

Integrate Workflow schema validation into resource listing/detail/update APIs once the core Workflow validator and validation error schema exist.

title: Define core Workflow YAML schema v1 node types
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403. Supports pi-agent-orchestrator-6bea.

Define the minimal v1 Workflow YAML node type set before expanding schema/editor/runtime scope.

Open design question:
- Which node types are truly needed for v1 versus future expansion?

Candidate minimal v1 types:
- root: top-level start/synthesis agent or orchestrator entry.
- agent: spawned/active agent role or template.
- gate: validation/human/approval decision point.

Possible deferred types:
- phase: may be represented by labels/groups initially.
- artifact/handoff: may be represented as handoff schema rather than node.
- human approval: may be a gate subtype initially.

Acceptance:

- Provide small YAML examples.
- Update downstream schema/editor/skill assumptions.

title: Add Live Agents WorkflowRun runtime overlay
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403.

Add dashboard visibility for active WorkflowRuns in or near the Live Agents panel, including actual running agent topology and user interventions.

Decisions captured:
- Static Workflow editor Mermaid previews show what may happen.
- Live Agents runtime overlay should show what is actually running now.
- User-to-agent messages for v1 happen through Live Agents direct messaging, not special prompt syntax.

Acceptance:

- Show user dashboard messages/interventions on the run timeline.

title: Define WorkflowRun failure timeout and escalation behavior
description:
Part of pi-agent-orchestrator-0403.

Define how WorkflowRun runtime handles child failures, timeouts, exhausted loops, cancelled agents, and escalation back to parent or root.

Acceptance:
